1: Type. "A wise woman builds up her household" — Christ is the one who builds his household. "I will build my church" (Matthew 16:18) applies house-building to the community of the redeemed; the Pauline image of the church as "God's building" with Christ as the foundation (1 Corinthians 3:9–11) and the household of God "built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ Jesus himself as the chief cornerstone" (Ephesians 2:20–22) fulfills the wise-builder image. Folly that tears down its own house is the counterpart of apostasy that abandons the cornerstone.
2: Revelation of God. "Whoever walks in uprightness fears the LORD" — Christ is the one human being who walked in perfect uprightness, whose fear of the LORD was not the trembling of guilt but the glad deference of the beloved Son to the Father. Isaiah describes the messianic figure as one on whom "the Spirit of the LORD" rests, including "the Spirit of the fear of the LORD" (Isaiah 11:2–3). Christ's uprightness and reverence for the Father is the lived reality that this proverb articulates as the proper pattern of human existence before God.
3: Theme. "The lips of the wise protect them" — Christ's wise speech consistently protected not only himself but those around him from spiritual harm. His rebuke of Peter ("Get behind me, Satan!" Matthew 16:23) and his silencing of demons (Mark 1:25) are acts of protective speech. In Gethsemane, when asked if he is Jesus of Nazareth, he identifies himself and the soldiers fall back (John 18:6) — his word exercises protective power. His speech as the Logos is the ultimate instance of words that "protect."
4: Theme. "Where there are no oxen, the stall is clean, but much increase comes through the strength of an ox" — the productive mess of genuine work is the principle behind Christ's willingness to enter the contamination of human existence. The incarnation is the supreme version of the hard-work principle: the stall of creation became "dirty" (groaning, broken, death-filled) when God entered it in Christ, but "much increase" — resurrection life, reconciled creation, the harvest of the kingdom — comes only through this costly, strength-filled engagement. The clean but empty stall is the world without the incarnation.
5: Type. "An honest witness does not lie" — Christ is the faithful and true witness, the final embodiment of truthful testimony. Revelation applies to him the title "the faithful and true witness" (ho martys ho pistos kai alēthinos, Revelation 3:14; Proverbs 19:11), echoing the language of faithful testimony that Proverbs consistently upholds. His testimony about himself ("I am the way, the truth, and the life," John 14:6) is the most far-reaching truth-claim any witness has ever made — and he authenticates it through resurrection.
6: Theme. "Knowledge comes easily to a person of understanding" — Christ gives wisdom to those whose hearts are prepared to receive it, while those who approach him with hard hearts ("lest they see with their eyes, hear with their ears, and understand with their hearts and turn," Matthew 13:15) remain in their blindness. His explanation: "To you it has been given to know the secrets of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it has not been given" (Matthew 13:11) — not arbitrary withholding but the recognition that understanding is the condition for receiving further understanding.
7: Theme. "Stay away from a foolish person — there is no knowledge on his lips" — Paul's warnings about "foolish controversies" (Titus 3:9), "the foolish and ignorant arguments" that produce quarrels (2 Timothy 2:23), and his instruction to "have nothing to do with the fruitless deeds of darkness" (Ephesians 5:11) apply this proverb ecclesially. Christ himself practiced selective engagement: he answered the Pharisees' trick questions only to expose their foolishness (Matthew 22:15–46) and then stopped engaging them when it became clear no learning was intended.
8: Revelation of God. "The wisdom of the prudent is to understand his way" — Christ is the Way who understands himself as Way (John 14:6). His perfect self-knowledge — "I know where I came from and where I am going" (John 8:14) — is the fullest possible expression of this proverb's principle. The prudent person's wisdom is to understand their particular path; Christ's wisdom is to BE the path that all paths are to understand. His self-understanding is not a model for imitation but the ontological ground that makes all human self-understanding possible.
9: Type. "Fools scoff at making amends for sin, but among the upright there is goodwill" — the "making amends" (ʾāšām, guilt offering, reparation) points toward the sacrificial system that Christ fulfills. He is both the guilt-offering that makes amends and the upright one among whom goodwill dwells. Hebrews 10:4–10 contrasts the inadequate sacrifices of the old system (scoffed at by fools as insufficient anyway) with Christ's once-for-all offering that actually deals with sin. The "goodwill" (rāṣôn, pleasure, acceptance) among the upright is the divine favor that rests on the Son (Matthew 3:17).
10: Revelation of God. "Each heart knows its own sorrow, and no outsider can fully share its joy" — Christ enters the interiority that no human being can fully enter. He who is "familiar with suffering" (Isaiah 53:3) knows each heart's particular grief not as an outside observer but as the incarnate one who has taken up human sorrow as his own (Matthew 8:17, citing Isaiah 53:4). The sorrow that no outsider can share is shared by the one who is not merely an outsider: "we do not have a high priest who is unable to empathize with our weaknesses" (Hebrews 4:15).
11: Theme. "The household of the wicked will be ruined, but the tent of the upright will flourish" — the flourishing tent (ʾōhel) of the upright echoes Israel's tabernacle-dwelling, the tent of God's presence. Christ's body is the true temple/tent of God's dwelling: "The Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen, tabernacled) among us" (John 1:14). The flourishing of the upright's tent is the flourishing of the community that becomes God's dwelling place through the Spirit (Ephesians 2:22). The wicked's ruined house corresponds to the old house of stone temple that was torn down (Matthew 24:2).
12: Direct. "There is a path that seems right to a person, but it leads to death in the end" — Jesus responds to this verse directly: "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). The many paths that seem right but lead to death are the alternative to the one true Way. His statement does not merely add Christ to the list of wise teachers — it claims that he alone is the referent of all true way-following, and that all other ways, however plausible, are the paths-toward-death that Proverbs identifies here.
13: Revelation of God. "Even in laughter the heart may ache, and the end of joy can be sorrow" — Christ, who was "despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief" (Isaiah 53:3), knew this depth of human experience. His weeping at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:35) in the face of the disciples' professed faith, his "loud cries and tears" in Gethsemane (Hebrews 5:7), and his cry of dereliction (Mark 15:34) show the deep ache beneath the surface. He enters human grief fully, making the joy-that-ends-in-sorrow his own in order to transform it into sorrow-that-ends-in-joy (John 16:20).
14: Theme. "One who turns away from faithfulness is filled with the results of his own choices" — the gospel's logic of bearing one's own consequences is applied in reverse to Christ: he who had no faithlessness to bear bore our results as if they were his own. "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us" (2 Corinthians 5:21) — he received the consequences of faithlessness without having been faithless. This exchange (he bearing what belongs to us; we receiving what belongs to him) is the heart of Paul's atonement theology, presupposed by this verse's consequence-logic.
15: Theme. "The prudent person gives careful thought to his steps" — Christ exercises perfect discernment about timing, audience, and engagement. He does not heal publicly when secrecy is appropriate (Mark 1:44); he does not answer when silence is wiser (Mark 14:61); he does not go to Jerusalem ahead of time (John 7:6–8). The "careful thought to his steps" that Proverbs commends to the prudent is embodied in Christ's deliberate, unhurried navigation of his own earthly mission — he is never reactive, always knowing the step he is taking and why.
16: Revelation of God. "A wise man fears and turns from evil" — Christ's perfect fear of God is the model of this verse. Isaiah 11:3 describes the messianic Spirit's gift as "the Spirit of the fear of the LORD"; the servant who "delights in the fear of the LORD" is the coming king who turns from evil with perfect consistency. In Gethsemane, Christ's "reverent submission" (eulabeia, Hebrews 5:7) — his God-fearing — coexisted with full acceptance of the Father's will. His fear was not terror but the deep reverential submission that is the disposition of perfect wisdom.
17: Theme. "A quick-tempered person acts foolishly, and a person who schemes wickedly is despised" — Christ demonstrates the opposite of both: in the face of arrest, trial, and mockery he exhibits the "slow to anger" that Proverbs consistently attributes to wisdom (Proverbs 14:29; Proverbs 16:32). James 1:19–20 grounds the practical prohibition on quick anger in the nature of "the righteousness of God" — the character of Christ who never schemed, reacted rashly, or allowed anger to drive his actions. His patience under provocation is the lived form of this proverb's positive counterpart.
18: Theme. "The prudent are crowned with knowledge" — the crowning of the wise with knowledge is an eschatological image that points toward the crowns of the faithful (2 Timothy 4:8; Revelation 2:10; James 1:12). Christ first receives the crown of thorns as a mockery (Matthew 27:29) and then the diadem of many crowns as the glorified Lord (Revelation 19:12). His path — from the crown of shame to the crown of glory — is the paradigm of the wisdom-trajectory: the one who truly possesses knowledge is vindicated and crowned in the end.
19: Theme. "Evil people will bow before those who are good, and the wicked at the gates of the righteous" — this is realized eschatologically in Philippians 2:10–11: "at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord." The proverb describes a reversal of present power arrangements; Paul applies it to the cosmic acknowledgment of Christ's lordship that is the final destiny of all creation — including those who opposed him. The "gates of the righteous" become the judgment seat before which all must appear.
20: Theme. "Even his neighbor dislikes a poor person, but a rich person has many friends" — Christ becomes poor to identify with the disliked, friendless poor. In his earthly ministry he deliberately associates with the marginalized, tax collectors, sinners, and the socially isolated, reversing the social logic this proverb describes. The disciples who abandoned him at his arrest (Matthew 26:56) confirm the proverb's observation even in his own experience. But Christ is also the "friend of sinners" (Matthew 11:19) who befriends the poor precisely when no one else will.
21: Direct. "Blessed is whoever shows kindness to the poor" — Jesus identifies kindness to the poor as the criterion of eschatological judgment: "Whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine, you did for me" (Matthew 25:40). The blessedness promised here (ʾašrêw) is the same beatitude-language that Jesus deploys in the Sermon on the Mount for the poor themselves (Matthew 5:3). Christ himself is simultaneously the poor one who receives kindness and the Lord who rewards those who showed it.
22: Theme. "Those who plan good find loyal love and faithfulness" (ḥesed wəʾĕmet) — the covenantal pair hesed and ʾĕmet (loving-kindness and faithfulness) that characterizes God's relationship with Israel is what those who plan good receive. Christ is the one in whom these covenant attributes are finally embodied: "grace and truth" (charis kai alētheia, John 1:14) are the NT parallel. Those who are "in Christ" — the one whose whole plan was the good of human redemption — receive the full covenantal blessing that this verse promises to those who plan good.
23: Theme. "There is profit in all hard work" — Christ's redemptive work is the supreme instance of the hard-labor principle. The author of Hebrews describes the cross as an act of purposeful endurance: "for the joy set before him he endured the cross" (Hebrews 12:2) — profit through costly labor. The kingdom he wins through his suffering is the harvest of his hard work. "The Lord of the harvest" (Matthew 9:38) first goes through the harvest's labor himself before sending workers; all kingdom productivity flows from his prior costly engagement.
24: Theme. "The wealth of the wise is their crown" — Paul identifies Christ as the wisdom (sophia) of God (1 Corinthians 1:24, 30) and those in Christ as "heirs of God and co-heirs with Christ" (Romans 8:17), sharing his inheritance. The crown that wisdom generates — the royal dignity of the truly wise — is Christ's to give to those who share his wisdom through union with him. James promises "the crown of life" to those who love God (James 1:12); Peter promises "the crown of glory that will never fade" (1 Peter 5:4) — crowns distributed by the chief Shepherd who himself wears the diadem of glory.
25: Type. "A truthful witness saves lives, but a false witness deals in lies" — the stakes of honest testimony reach their highest point in Christ's testimony on trial. He is the truthful witness who saves lives even at the cost of his own: his honest confession that he is "the Son of God" (Matthew 26:63–64) before the Sanhedrin secures his condemnation but saves those who will believe his testimony. The false witnesses who testify against him (Matthew 26:60–61) embody the deadly damage that false witness does — but their lies cannot undo the life-saving power of his truth.
26: Theme. "The fear of the LORD is a source of strong confidence, and his children will find refuge in him" — Christ as the one in whom God's children find their refuge. Paul identifies believers as those who have received "the Spirit of adoption to sonship. And by him we cry, 'Abba, Father'" (Romans 8:15) — the filial access to divine refuge that this verse promises. Christ is simultaneously the strong confidence (he in whom we trust) and the model of confidence (his Gethsemane prayer is the supreme example of fearful-reverent trust in the Father).
27: Allusion. "The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life, turning one away from the traps of death" — identical in substance to Proverbs 13:14 ("the teaching of the wise is a fountain of life"), confirming that fear-of-YHWH and wisdom are interchangeable. Christ, who is identified as the fountain of living water (John 4:14; Proverbs 7:38), is the source from which this fountain flows. The traps of death (moqəšê māwet) that wisdom turns one away from are the same bondages from which Christ releases: "the fear of death" that keeps people in "slavery all their lives" (Hebrews 2:15).
28: Type. "A large population is a king's glory" — Christ, the divine King, is glorified in the vastness of his people. "A great multitude that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people and language, standing before the throne" (Revelation 7:9) is the fulfillment of the royal glory that this proverb describes. The "ruined prince" of the second half points toward every alternative sovereignty — political, religious, ideological — whose decline in adherents signals its failure. Christ's glory increases as his people multiply across history.
29: Revelation of God. "Whoever is slow to anger shows great understanding" — Christ as the supreme embodiment of patience. Peter describes God's delay in judgment as patience: "He is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish" (2 Peter 3:9). Christ demonstrates this patience toward his disciples' repeated failures (Matthew 26:40–45) and toward the hostile crowds whose questioning he endures. His "great understanding" is the divine comprehension of human weakness — "for he knows how we are formed, he remembers that we are dust" (Psalms 103:14) — that grounds patience rather than quick anger.
30: Theme. "A calm and contented heart brings health to the body, but envy eats away at the bones" — the peace Christ offers (John 14:27, "my peace I give to you, not as the world gives") is the foundation of the calm heart that produces health. Paul's "peace of God, which transcends all understanding" that "will guard your hearts and minds" (Philippians 4:7) is the body-health that this proverb locates in inner peace. Envy as the destroyer corresponds to the "strife and selfish ambition" that Paul contrasts with love — the disintegrating passion that Christ's peace displaces.
31: Direct. "Whoever shows kindness to the needy honors him" — Christ identifies himself directly with the needy: "I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink" (Matthew 25:35). To show kindness to the needy is to honor God because God is present in the needy in a way that this proverb anticipates. Christ's identification with "the least of these" (Matthew 25:40) makes the Maker's presence in the poor literally visible in the incarnate Son who becomes poor (2 Corinthians 8:9) to stand beside those who have nothing.
32: Type. "The righteous have hope even in death" — the resurrection of Christ is the supreme fulfillment of this verse. His hope in death was vindicated on the third day; his resurrection is the first installment of the "righteous have hope even in death" promise extended to all who are in him. Paul grounds resurrection hope in Christ's: "For since we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so, through Jesus, God will bring with him those who have fallen asleep" (1 Thessalonians 4:14). The righteous person's hope in death is not wishful thinking but a consequence of union with the one who proved that death is not the end.
33: Theme. "Wisdom quietly rests in the heart of the discerning" — Paul describes wisdom's indwelling in terms of Christ's presence: "Christ in you, the hope of glory" (Colossians 1:27). The "wisdom and knowledge" hidden in Christ (Colossians 2:3) is the fulfillment of the wise person's quiet wisdom-rest. Wisdom no longer merely resides in the discerning heart as a quality cultivated through formation; it dwells there as the person of Christ through the Spirit — a personalized, relational presence of the wisdom who is also a person.
34: Theme. "Righteousness lifts a nation up, but sin brings disgrace to any people" — Christ as the source of national righteousness. The OT vision of a righteous nation is fulfilled in the church as a "holy nation" (1 Peter 2:9), and ultimately in the eschatological kingdom where "the nation of the saved" walks by the light of the Lamb (Revelation 21:24). Christ's righteousness imputed to his people is the only basis for the corporate righteousness that lifts a community beyond shame. The nations's sin-disgrace is overcome only by participation in the Righteous One.
35: Type. "A king's favor rests on a servant who acts wisely" — Isaiah's Servant who "acted wisely" (yaskîl, Isaiah 52:13, the same word as śekel in this verse) and therefore receives the favor/exaltation of the King is the direct fulfillment of this proverb's pattern. Christ as the Servant of the LORD who "acted wisely" in his obedient redemptive mission receives the highest royal favor: "Therefore God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name" (Philippians 2:9). The king's favor on the wise servant is the type; Christ's exaltation is the antitype.
